perturbation.original_text: The undersigned will hold Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not, nor will it permit any agent, servant or employee to, copy, reproduce, sell, assign, license, market, transfer or otherwise dispose of, give or disclose Confidential Information to any person, firm or corporation including any partner or employee of Goldman Sachs who does not have a need to know the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned will endeavor to hold Confidential Information in confidence and will not knowingly permit any agent, servant or employee to copy, reproduce, sell, assign, license, market, transfer or otherwise dispose of, give or disclose Confidential Information to any outside person, firm or corporation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will hold in strict confidence' to 'will endeavor to hold in confidence' weakens the obligation. The addition of 'knowingly' introduces a subjective element, and limiting disclosure to 'outside' entities creates ambiguity about internal disclosures, thus creating an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute prohibits government employees (which could arguably extend to contractors in some contexts, depending on their role) from disclosing confidential information. The modified text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, potentially allowing for disclosures that would be prohibited under the statute, especially concerning the 'endeavor' and 'knowingly' conditions. Even if the person *endeavors* to keep it a secret, they may still leak the information.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next Whoever, being an officer or employee of the United States or of any department or agency thereof, any person acting on behalf of the Federal Housing Finance Agency , or agent of the Department of Justice as defined in the Antitrust Civil Process Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1311–131 4), or being an employee of a private sector organization who is or was assigned to an agency under chapter 37 of title 5, publishes, divulges, discloses, or makes known in any manner or to any extent not authorized by law any information coming to him in the course of his employment or official duties or by reason of any examination or investigation made by, or return, report or record made to or filed with, such department or agency or officer or employee thereof, which information concerns or relates to the trade secrets, processes, operations, style of work, or apparatus, or to the identity, confidential statistical data, amount or source of any income, profits, losses, or expenditures of any person, firm, partnership, corporation, or association; or permits any income return or copy thereof or any book containing any abstract or particulars thereof to be seen or examined by any person except as provided by law; shall be fined under this title, or imprisoned not more than one year, or both; and shall be removed from office or employment. (June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 791 ; Pub. L. 96–349, § 7(b) , Sept. 12, 1980 , 94 Stat. 1158 ; Pub. L. 102–550, title XIII, § 1353 , Oct. 28, 1992 , 106 Stat. 3970 ; Pub. L. 104–294, title VI, § 601(a)(8) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3498 ; Pub. L. 107–347, title II, § 209(d)(2) , Dec. 17, 2002 , 116 Stat. 2930 ; Pub. L. 110–289, div. A, title I, §
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates strict confidentiality, aligning with the aim of 18 U.S. Code § 1905 to prevent unauthorized disclosure of confidential information by government employees or those acting on their behalf. The modified text, however, weakens this obligation by changing 'will hold in strict confidence' to 'will endeavor to hold in confidence,' introducing a subjective standard. The addition of 'knowingly' further diminishes the responsibility, as it requires proof of intent for a breach to occur. Furthermore, limiting disclosure to 'outside' entities creates ambiguity about internal disclosures. This directly contradicts the purpose of the law, which seeks to prevent any unauthorized disclosure, regardless of intent or recipient, as detailed in the law snippet: 'Whoever... publishes, divulges, discloses, or makes known in any manner or to any extent not authorized by law...'. The modifications allow for potential breaches that would be prohibited under the original contract and the statute, constituting a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of the services to be performed by the undersigned (or earlier if requested by Goldman Sachs), the undersigned shall return to Goldman Sachs all copies of documents, papers or other material relating to Goldman Sachs or obtained or developed in the course of performing services for Goldman Sachs, or containing or derived from Confidential Information which are in the undersigned's possession, together, if requested by Goldman Sachs, with a certificate signed by the undersigned, in form and substance satisfactory to Goldman Sachs, to the effect that all such Confidential Information has been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of the services to be performed by the undersigned, the undersigned should return documents relating to Goldman Sachs that are readily available. A certificate may be requested.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall return all copies' to 'should return documents that are readily available' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. Removing the specifics about 'papers or other material,' 'obtained or developed in the course of performing services', and 'containing or derived from Confidential Information' narrows the scope vaguely. Furthermore, changing the mandatory certificate to 'may be requested' makes compliance discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly addressing document return, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act emphasizes the importance of accurate and reliable financial reporting and internal controls. The original text supports these objectives through its stringent requirements of proper records disposal to prevent things like the destruction or manipulation of financial records. The modified text weakens internal controls related to document management and creates a significant ambiguity that can violate the record-keeping spirit and goals of the act, especially for Goldman Sachs.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates the return of 'all copies of documents' and a signed certificate, supporting thorough record disposal. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the undersigned 'should return documents that are readily available' and that a 'certificate may be requested'. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the scope of required documentation, potentially impacting compliance with regulations concerning accurate financial reporting, especially for Goldman Sachs as highlighted in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned agrees to provide any assistance necessary to Goldman Sachs in investigating any illegal or fraudulent activities, security breaches or similar situations.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned will consider providing assistance to Goldman Sachs in investigating potentially illegal activities, security breaches or similar situations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to provide any assistance necessary' to 'will consider providing assistance' significantly weakens the obligation, making it discretionary. The addition of 'potentially' before 'illegal activities' further reduces the obligation by introducing uncertainty. The legal obligation becomes highly ambiguous and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute requires individuals with knowledge of a felony to report it to authorities. By stating that the undersigned will only 'consider' providing assistance, the modified text creates an ambiguous obligation that could impede investigations. While the contractor isn't necessarily directly committing misprision of felony by not reporting, this clause aims to ensure cooperation, and its weakening undermines the ability of Goldman Sachs to investigate thoroughly and potentially report crimes, thus potentially violating the *spirit* of the law, which encourages reporting of federal crimes.

Misprision of felony Whoever, having knowledge of the actual commission of a felony cognizable by a court of the United States, conceals and does not as soon as possible make known the same to some judge or other person in civil or military authority under the United States, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than three years, or both. ( June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 684 ; Pub. L. 103–322, title XXXIII, §330016(1)(G), Sept. 13, 1994, 108 Stat. 214

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to provide assistance by using "will consider providing assistance" instead of "agrees to provide any assistance necessary." The original text created a mandatory obligation, while the modified text makes the assistance discretionary. According to 18 U.S. Code § 4, anyone having knowledge of a felony must report it. The law snippets state: "Whoever, having knowledge of the actual commission of a felony cognizable by a court of the United States, conceals and does not as soon as possible make known the same to some judge or other person... shall be fined... or imprisoned... or both." By changing the language to 'consider providing assistance,' the contract introduces ambiguity as to whether the individual will actually report a felony, thus undermining the spirit, if not the letter, of the law that encourages the reporting of federal crimes.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned agrees that in connection with performing the services contemplated by this Agreement, he or she may be subject to a background check, including employers, education, credit, criminal public record, drug screen or other checking or testing, and the undersigned consents to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned acknowledges that a background check may be conducted, and consents to providing readily available information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specifics of the background check (employers, education, etc.) and limiting consent to 'readily available information' introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what information is considered readily available. This ambiguity makes the extent of the background check unclear and gives the employee leeway to withhold crucial information, contradicting FCRA guidelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCRA regulates background checks and requires employers to obtain clear and conspicuous consent for specific checks. The original clause ensures this consent. However, by removing the specifics of the background check and limiting consent to 'readily available information,' the modified clause violates FCRA requirements for informed and explicit consent. Employers must disclose the nature and scope of the background check, and the employee must willingly give their consent, instead of just handing over readily available information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by introducing ambiguity regarding the scope of the background check and limiting consent to 'readily available information'. This undermines the FCRA's requirement for clear and conspicuous consent for specific background checks. The original text listed specific checks, which the modified text removes, making it unclear what the employee is consenting to. Scraped Law Snippet 1 alludes to amendments and regulations regarding consumer reporting agencies, indicating the importance of compliance in this domain. The change makes it difficult to enforce the background check process, potentially violating FCRA guidelines.
